comment_text.sn35.156:1.1: This discourse and the next are similar to SN 22.51.
comment_text.sn35.156:1.3: “Relishing” (nandi) and “greed” (or “lust”, rāga) are similar in meaning, but with distinct stresses: “relishing” enjoys the object of desire, while “greed” is inflamed by passion for it.

root_text.sn35.156:1.1: “Aniccaṁyeva, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cakkhuṁ aniccanti passati, sāssa hoti sammādiṭṭhi.
root_text.sn35.156:1.6: aniccaṁyeva, bhikkhave, bhikkhu jivhaṁ aniccanti passati, sāssa hoti sammādiṭṭhi.
root_text.sn35.156:1.11: aniccaṁyeva, bhikkhave, bhikkhu manaṁ aniccanti passati, sāssa hoti sammādiṭṭhi.

translation_text.sn35.156:0.3: The Interior and the End of Relishing
translation_text.sn35.156:1.1: “Mendicants, the eye really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view.
translation_text.sn35.156:1.3: When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends.
translation_text.sn35.156:1.5: When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed.
translation_text.sn35.156:1.11: mind really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view.
translation_text.sn35.156:1.13: When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends.
translation_text.sn35.156:1.15: When relishing and greed end, the mind is said to be well freed.”
